coredata.dc:title: A novel SERS sensor for the ultrasensitive detection of kanamycin based on a Zn-doped carbon quantum dot catalytic switch controlled by nucleic acid aptamer and size-controlled gold nanorods
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ximo
Chen, Chen
Waterhouse, Geoffrey I.N.
Qiao, Xuguang
Xu, Zhixiang
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) sensor was developed for the ultrasensitive determination of kanamycin in foods. The sensor used two distinct signal amplification strategies, namely the surface plasmon resonance of gold nanorods and a Zn-doped carbon quantum dots catalytic cascade oxidation-reduction reaction switch controlled by a nucleic acid aptamer. Under optimized experimental conditions, the SERS sensor demonstrated a linear range of 10−12 to 10−5 g mL−1 for the detection of kanamycin, with a limit of detection of 3.03 × 10−13 g mL−1. Experiments with antibiotics structurally similar to kanamycin and interferrants revealed that the sensor had excellent selectivity. Milkpowder and honey samples spiked with kanamycin were assayed, with recoveries ranging from 84.1% to 107.2% and a relative standard deviation of 0.74% to 2.81% being obtained. Quantification of kanamycin in milk samples revealed no significant difference between the results obtained with the sensor and by HPLC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Catalytic switch
Metal-doped carbon quantum dots
Kanamycin
Plasmon resonance
Raman substrate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Establishment of doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method
Synthesis of gold nanorods with the desired aspect ratio
Evaluation of doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method
Accuracy and applicability of the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–visible-NIR absorbance spectra for AuNRs at different reaction times after adding HAuCl4·3H2O. As the reaction time increased, the Au NRs became progressively shorter.
SEM image of the AuNRs prepared at a reaction time of 7.5 h.

Effect of the concentration of HAuCl4 (A), GLC (C), and the amount of aptamer (E) on the Raman intensities of the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor. Plots (B), (D) and (F) show plots of Intensity and 495 cm−1 versus the parameter varies in (A), (C) and (E), respectively.
Linear relationship between the Raman intensity at 495 cm−1 and the negative logarithm of the kanamycin concentration in the range 10−12 to 10−5 g mL−1.
Selectivity of the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor (495 cm−1) towards kanamycin and other antibiotics.
Recoveries of kanamycin from spiked samples using the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method.

head.ce:title: A novel SERS sensor for the ultrasensitive detection of kanamycin based on a Zn-doped carbon quantum dot catalytic switch controlled by nucleic acid aptamer and size-controlled gold nanorods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province, College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian 271018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province
College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province, College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian 271018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, The University of Auckland, Auckland 1142, New Zealand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, The University of Auckland, Auckland 1142, New Zealand

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Chen Chen and Ximo Wang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) sensor was developed for the ultrasensitive determination of kanamycin in foods. The sensor used two distinct signal amplification strategies, namely the surface plasmon resonance of gold nanorods and a Zn-doped carbon quantum dots catalytic cascade oxidation-reduction reaction switch controlled by a nucleic acid aptamer. Under optimized experimental conditions, the SERS sensor demonstrated a linear range of 10−12 to 10−5 g mL−1 for the detection of kanamycin, with a limit of detection of 3.03 × 10−13 g mL−1. Experiments with antibiotics structurally similar to kanamycin and interferrants revealed that the sensor had excellent selectivity. Milkpowder and honey samples spiked with kanamycin were assayed, with recoveries ranging from 84.1% to 107.2% and a relative standard deviation of 0.74% to 2.81% being obtained. Quantification of kanamycin in milk samples revealed no significant difference between the results obtained with the sensor and by HPLC.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, antibiotic drug residues in meats and foods has become a concern for consumers. Kanamycin is currently one of the most widely used and effective antibiotics in the treatment of animal infectious diseases (Zhang et al., 2018), due to its ability to kill bacteria rapidly by interfering with protein synthesis in bacteria. However, long-term intake of meats, eggs, milk and other foods containing kanamycin residues can cause a range of health problems for humans including dizziness, nausea, tinnitus, increased drug resistance,kidney damage and auditory nerve damage (Economou & Gousia, 2015; Tian, Zhang, Wang, & Geng, 2020). Accordingly, sensitive methods are needed for the detection of kanamycin in foods and beverages.

Current assays for kanamycin use high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Wang, Yang, Zhu, & Yin, 2014; Zhang et al., 2015), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Dijkstra et al., 2014), microbiological methods (Ramezani, Danesh, Lavaee, Abnous, & Taghdisi, 2016), capillary electrophoresis (Long et al., 2003; Yu, He, Fu, Xie, & Gan, 2009), and the enzyme-linked immunosorbant assay (ELISA) (Li et al., 2017). HPLC and LC-MS are the most accurate and reliable methods for kanamycin quantification. However, due to the lack of a chromophore, pre-column or post-column derivatizations are needed for kanamycin detection. This adds significant complexity to the analysis. Microbiological methods and capillary electrophoresis techniques are highly efficient, but have high limits of detection and poor repeatability which limits their application. ELISA has the advantages of high specificity and sensitivity, but the preparation of antibodies is difficult and costly. Therefore, the fast detection of trace amounts of kanamycin in foods and beverages remains technically challenging, with improvements in detection sensitivity also required.

Surface Enhanced Raman Scattering (SERS) is a powerful technique for the detection of molecules and ions at low concentrations, and has obvious potential for the analytical determined of kanamycin in foods and beverages (Yan, Chen, She, Ma, Hong, Shao, Abd El-Aty, Wang, Wang, & Wang, 2019; Zheng, Ni, Yu, & Liang, 2017). In the development of SERS sensors, particular attention needs to be placed on the design a suitable substrate to generate large local electric fields under laser irradiation, thus amplifying the Raman signal of the analyte by many orders of magnitude leading to high detection sensitivity. These local electric fields are particularly intense when the Raman laser frequency matches the localized surface plasmon resonance (SPR) frequency of the plasmonic nanoparticles or plasmonic structures commonly used as SERS substrates (Meyer, Le Ru, & Etchegoin, 2011). When the SPR peak of the SERS substrate is resonantly coupled with the incident laser light, SERS enhancement factors as high as 1012 can be achieved (Long, Li, Du, Geng, & Liu, 2021; Nguyen, Zhang, Mustapha, Li, & Lin, 2014). In optimized cases, single molecule detection is possible. Compared with conventional spherical nanoparticles commonly used as SERS substrates, gold nanorods offer superior SERS enhancement effects (due to the presence of both transverse and longitudinal plasmon resonances) and better stability against aggregation (Alsammarraie et al., 2018; Chuang et al., 2014), thus delivering improved overall properties as a SERS substrate. Control of the size and shape of gold nanorods is important for maximizing SERS activity, with rods of particular aspect ratios (i.e. AR, with uniformity in width and length) being desirable. Various approaches have been reported for adjusting the length and thus the longitudinal SPR position of gold nanorods, including seed-growth methods using cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) for shape control, temperature control, laser induction, cyanide dissolution, and chemical etching (Burrows, Harvey, Idesis, & Murphy, 2017; Requejo, Liopo, Derry, & Zubarev, 2017; Shafeev et al., 2018). The three-step seed growth method and chemical dissolution methods are generally the most reliable for preparing gold nanorods with specific aspect ratios (Khanal & Zubarev, 2019). However the use of gold nanorods alone as Raman substrates has limitations in sensitivity and selectivity. Additional strategies are need to further boost the sensitivity and selectivity of Raman detection of target analytes, such as kanamycin.

With a view towards improving the properties of Au nanorods for SERS, researchers are exploring approaches for depositing Au atoms on the surface of the nanorods to increase surface roughness. For examples, the reduction chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) can be sued to deposit elemental gold atoms, further improve the sensitivity of Raman detection of analytes by SERS (Schwartzberg, Grant, Vanbuuren, & Zhang, 2007). The rate of Au deposition can be controlled by selecting a suitable catalyst. Semiconducting quantum dots (QDs) have very large specific surface areas and thus abundant surface active sites, making them suitable catalysts for many applications (Arvand & Mirroshandel, 2019; Chaudhary, Bansal, & Mehta, 2014). Further, doping with metals is a commonly used strategy to improve catalytic efficiency of QDs (Khan et al., 2019). Metal-doped QDs frequently exhibit a higher electron transfer efficiency than their un-doped counterparts, is beneficial for promoting charge carrier separation in semiconducting QDs. In turn, this is beneficial for photocatalytic and electrocatalytic reactions, such as the reduction of AuCl4 − ions to elemental gold atoms and nanoclusters. Adsorption of QDs on Au nanorods therefore represents a way of modifying the surface of the nanorods, with the catalytic activity of the QDs being used to control Au deposition via an oxidation–reduction process (Yang, Liu, Xu, Bai, & Hu, 2020).

In recent years, nucleic acid aptamers have been widely utilized in analytical technique development due to their high selectivity an specific recognition ability towards specific analytes. Sabet et al. combined the specific recognition of aptamer with the fluorescence quenching effect of QDs to develop an aflatoxin sensor with the limit of detection of 3.4 nM (Sabet, Hosseini, Khabbaz, Dadmehr, & Ganjali, 2017). Tan et al. developed a double recognition sensor based on aptamers and a molecular imprinted polymer for cytochrome c detection, with the vinyl-modified Mn-doped ZnS QDs acting as the signal source. Cytochrome c detection with a linear concentration range of 0.2–2.0 μmol L−1 was achieved (Tan et al., 2018). Switch structures controlled by nucleic acid aptamers have been shown to improve the selectivity of SERS-based Raman sensors, allowing higher analyte sensitivity (Li & Zhao, 2020; Shoara et al., 2017).

Embracing the aforementioned principles, we sought here to develop a novel SERS sensor for the analytical detection of kanamycin. Our approach was based on plasmonic gold nanorods as the Raman substrate (with the longitudinal SPR resonantly coupled with incident 785 nm laser excitation), with cascade oxidation–reduction reaction catalytic switch controlled by nucleic acid aptamer being used to boost the analytical signal. For the “catalytic switch”, Zn-doped CQDs were prepared, whose catalytic activity was inhibited by nucleic acid aptamers spirally wrapped around the surface in the absence of the target (Freeman, Girsh, & Willner, 2013). On aptamer binding with kanamycin, the Zn-doped CQDs were released giving rise to catalytic activity for the reduction of Au3+ to Au on the surface of the Au nanorods, which boosted the SERS signal of kanamycin. This double signal amplification strategy allowed ultrasensitive SERS detection of kanamycin. Various factors that affect the performance of the sensor were optimized, and the selectivity and practical applicability of the analytical method evaluated.
Milk, honey and milkpowder were purchased from Tai’an RT-Mart (Taian, China) in October 2020.

Kanamycin (>99%), gentamycin (>98%), amikacin (>99%), and vancomycin (>98%) were obtained from by Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium citrate, citric acid, zinc chloride, glucose (GLC), ethanol, glycerol, copper chloride dihydrate, choline chloride, and ascorbic acid were purchased from Tianjin Kaitong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Calcium hydroxide was supplied by Tianjin Ouboke Chemical Reagent Products Sales Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Manganese carbonate, glycine, l-cysteine, trifluoroaceticacid, and methanol were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The trifluoroaceticacid and methanol were chromatographically pure, whilst the other reagents were analytically pure. Cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) was purchased from Beijing Biotopped Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), HAuCl4 and 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid (4-MPBA) were supplied by Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium borohydride was purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The water used in this experiment was doubly-distilled water (DDW, 18.2 ΜΩ cm), which was prepared using an Aike ultrapure water instrument (Chengdu, China).

Nucleic acid aptamers were supplied by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). They were diluted into 100 μmol L−1 in DDW and stored at −20 °C before use. The aptamer chain is as follow: 5′-TGGGGGTTGAGGCTAAGCCGA-3′.

Raman data were collected on a DXR2xi microscopic Raman imaging spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd, Waltham, America) equipped with DXR 785 nm source. The laser power used during the experiments was 2.0 mW, the an exposure time of 4.0 sec. Two scans were accumulated to produce a spectrum. The gold nanorods were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (S4800, Hitachi High-Technologies Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) and UV–visible spectrophotometry (UV-2450, Shimadzu Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd, ShimanePrefecture, Japan). FT-IR spectra of the metal-doped carbon QDs were collected on a Nicolet IS10 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. Fluorescence measurements used a Lumina fluorescence spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd, Waltham, America) operating at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The cation solid phase extraction column was provided by Beijing Dikma Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). A Shimadzu LC-20AT liquid chromatography system equipped with an evaporative light scattering detector (ELSD-LTⅡ) and a C18 column (5 μm, 4.6 × 150 mm) was used to determine the concentrations of kanamycin in milk, honey and milk powder samples (Shimadzu Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd, ShimanePrefecture, Japan). The mobile phase was a 0.2 mol L−1 trifluoroacetic acid solution-methanol (98:2, v/v); the flow rate was 1.0 mL min−1; the column temperature was 30 °C, and the injection volume was 20 μL. The drift tube temperature of the ELSD was 65 °C, and the carrier gas pressure was 3.5 bar.

Gold nanorods of different lengths and longitudinal SPR positions were prepared according to literature methods (Khanal & Zubarev, 2019; Nikoobakht, 2003). Briefly, 5 mL of an aqueous 0.2 mol L−1 CTAB solution was mixed with 5 mL of an aqueous HAuCl4 solution (0.0005 mol L−1). Afterwards, 0.6 mL of NaBH4 (0.01 mol L−1) was added under constant stirring to form a brownish yellow solution. The solution was stirred vigorously for 2 min, and then incubated in a water bath at 25 °C for 2 h (thus giving a Au seed solution). Separately, three test tubes (labeled A, B, C) contained 2.5 × 10−4 mol L−1 HAuCl4 (4.5 mL), 0.1 mol L−1 CTAB (4.5 mL) and 0.1 mol L−1 ascorbic acid solution (0.05 mL), respectively, were prepared. Seed solution (1 mL) was added to test tube A. After 4 h, 1 mL of the solution in test tube A and added to test tube B, followed by thorough mixing. After 4 h, 1 mL of solution was taken test tube B and added to test tube C. The resulting solution was then left overnight at room temperature, resulting in the formation of gold nanorods.

The chemical dissolution method was used to reduce the aspect ratio (AR) of the gold nanorods, thus allowing precise control the longitudinal SPR position. Briefly, 3.64 g of CTAB and 66 mg of HAuCl4·3H2O were dissolved in 100 mL DDW and the resulting solution stirred for 10 min. Next, 1 mL of this solution was added to 5 mL of the gold nanorods dispersion, and the gold nanorods were dissolved at 38 °C for 2–12.5 h. After reaction, the dispersion was centrifuged at 5900 × g for 10 min to remove the excess CTAB and dissolved ions. After washing with water, the modified Au nanorods were redispersed in 5 mL of DDW. By varying the dissolution time in the range 2–12.5 h, Au nanorods of different aspect ratios were obtained, with AR decreasing with increasing dissolution time.

The Zn-doped CQDs, Ca-doped CQDs, Mn-doped CQDs and Cu-doped CQDs were all synthesized according to reported literature methods (Wei, Wei, Huang, Guo, & Wang, 2020; Xu et al., 2018). The Zn-doped CQDs were prepared as follows. Sodium citrate (0.735 g) and zinc chloride (0.1704 g) were added into a beaker containing 25 mL of DDW under constant stirring. After dissolution of the salts, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL teflon-line autoclave, and heated at 185 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was filtered through a 0.22 μM filter membrane and stored at 4 °C for further use. The syntheses of the Ca-doped CQDs, Mn-doped CQDs and Cu-doped CQDs are described in the supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method
ce:section.ce:para: Aptamer solution (100 μmol L−1, 120 μL) was added to a 5 mL test tube, to which 50 μL of a kanamycin standard solution (10−12 g mL−1, 10−11 g mL−1, 10−10 g mL−1, 10−9 g mL−1, 10−8 g mL−1, 10−7 g mL−1, 10−6 g mL−1, or 10−5 g mL−1) and 70 μL of the Zn-doped carbon CDs dispersion were added. After stirred for 15 min, 15 μmol L−1 of HAuCl4 (100 μL) and 0.1 mol L−1 of GLC (150 μL) were added and the resulting dispersion kept in a water bath at 65 °C for 30 min. Next, a 0.1 mmol L−1 4-MPBA solution (10 μL) was added to 1 mL of the aforementioned dispersion under vortex, with the resulting dispersion then being incubated at room temperature for 2 h. The dispersion was then centrifuged at 1475 × g for 10 min to remove the excess 4-MPBA. Here, 4-MPBA served as the Raman label. The collected nanoparticles were then redispersed in 1 mL DDW and mixed with 1 mL of a gold nanorod dispersion (the Raman substrate). The resulting dispersion was then heated at 38 °C for 2 h, after which Raman spectra were collected and the intensity of the peak at 495 cm−1 associated with 4-MPBA determined.

In order to test the feasibility of the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method, milk, honey and milkpowder samples were spiked with a kanamycin standard solution were prepared. The kanamycin content in each of the samples was determined before and after spiking by HPLC. For the HPLC and SERS analyses of the milk samples, 8 g of milk and 30 mL of a 5% phosphoric acid solution were placed in a 100 mL centrifuge tube. After that, the solution was vortexed for 10 min.A trichloroacetic acid (3 mL) was then added to remove the protein, and the resulting solution centrifuged at 944 × g for 10 min. The supernatant was collected. A cation solid phase extraction column was activated with methanol (5 mL) and DDW (5 mL). Next, the supernatant was passed through the column, with the column being flushed successively with a formic acid aqueous solution (3 mL) and then methanol (3 mL). Finally, a 5% ammoniated methanol solution (3 mL) was used as the eluent to elute the kanamycin from the column. After nitrogen drying, the eluent was redissolved in 1 mL of DDW and filtered through a 0.22 μM membrane prior to HPLC and SERS analysis.
The pretreatment procedure for the honey and milkpowder samples were similar to those described above for the milk samples, except that 1 g of honey or milk powder was dissolved in 8 mL DDW.

The ANOVA test was used to evaluate the differences between the mean values of results obtained from the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor and HPLC method using SAS software (SAS Institute, Cary, NC, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of gold nanorods with the desired aspect ratio
ce:section.ce:para: Our initial goal in the development of our a SERS sensor for kanamycin was the synthesis of gold nanorods with the longitudinal SPR ~ 785 nm in water (the excitation wavelength of laser used to excite the Raman spectra). By coupling the longitudinal SPR and laser excitation wavelength, the intensity of the SERS spectrum of adsorbed kanamycin was expected to be greatly enhanced. Fig. 1 shows the effect of the chemical dissolution time on optical properties of the gold nanorods. The as-prepared Au nanords showed transverse SPR and longitudinal SPR features at 492 nm and 1081 nm, respectively. The presence of these two SPR features confirmed a rod-like structure (Xue, Wu, Li, & Miao, 2020). After adding HAuCl4·3H2O solution, the longitudinal SPR showed a significant blue shift, while the transverse SPR showed no obvious change. The blue shift in the longitudinal SPR increased with dissolution time, indicating that the dissolution was occurring at the ends of gold nanorods with the width of gold nanorods not changing. As the longitudinal SPR blue-shifted, the feature also became less intense due to the lower Raman cross section. The reaction could be stopped at any time during the dissolution stage by centrifuging the system. This allowed the longitudinal SPR position of gold nanorods to be precisely matched to the Raman laser (785 nm). When the dissolution time was 7.5 h, the position of longitudinal SPR peak was ~ 785 nm (Fig. 1). Accordingly, that batch of Au nanorods was used as Raman substrates in the development of the SERS sensor for kanamycin. The inset in Fig. 1 shows a digital photograph of the various Au nanorod dispersions, showing distinct color changes as the longitudinal SPR blue-shifted with increasing dissolution time.

Four different kinds of common metal-doped CQDs were synthesized in this work, with the aim of determining which one offered the best catalytic activity for signal amplification in our SERS sensor. The pH stability of the four different types of QDs was first investigated, with the stability study employing the characteristic fluorescence of CQDs ~ 440 nm under UV excitation as a guide. As shown in Fig. S1(A), the fluorescence intensity of the Cu-doped CQDs was low and decreased rapidly above pH 8.0 (likely due to aggregation or Cu oxide/hydroxide formation). The Ca-doped CQDs and Mn-doped CQDs showed intense fluorescence at some pH, but their fluorescence intensities varied considerably with pH making them not particularly useful for sensor construction. Conversely, the Zn-doped CQDs maintained a high fluorescence intensity and stable fluorescence over a wide pH range, leading to their use as catalysts in the development of the SERS sensor.

After selecting Zn-doped CQDs for the sensor development, further experiments were conducted attempting to optimize the synthesis of the Zn-doped CQDs for optimal performance. Fig. S1(B) shows the effect of hydrothermal synthesis time at 165 °C on the fluorescence intensity of Zn-doped CQDs under 360 nm excitation. The fluorescence intensity was maximized at a reaction time of 4 h, thus a time of 4 h was used in subsequent experiments. The effect of the hydrothermal synthesis temperature on the fluorescence properties of the Zn-doped CQDs was also investigated (Fig. S1(C)). Results showed that the emission peak position of the Zn-doped CQDs (440 nm) did not change with temperature, though the fluorescence intensity showed a strong temperature dependence on the hydrothermal synthesis temperature (Fig. S1(D)). A synthesis temperature of 185 °C yielded the most fluorescent Zn-doped CQDs. The decrease in the fluorescent intensity above 185 °C may be due to larger Zn-doped CQDs forming, or alternatively the loss of surface of surface ligands leading to the fluorescence quenching (Li & Li, 2014). Based on the data in Fig. S1(D), 185 °C was selected as the optimal temperature for Zn-doped CQDs synthesis.

The gold nanorods used in the development of the SERS sensor for kanamycin had a longitudinal SPR position of 785 nm in water, prepared at dissolution time of 7.5 h (Fig. 1). Fig. 2 shows a SEM of that sample. The nanorods in the sample have an average aspect ratio of 11:1, with a mean length ~ 165 nm and a width around 15 nm (Fig. 2). Results showed that the dissolution process leads to rods of quite uniform length and width.

Next, the stability of the Zn-doped CQDS was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S1(E), the fluorescence spectra under 360 excitation did not change significantly over the first three weeks of storage, with>90% of the initial fluorescence intensity being retained. After that, the fluorescence decreased and was completely quenched after eight weeks. Thus, it is concluded that the Zn-doped CQDs had good initial stability. Owing to the their ease of synthesis, they represent suitable catalysts for SERS sensor signal amplification. The FT-IR spectrum of Zn-doped CQDs (Fig. S2) showed intense peaks at 1580 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1, which correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups (citrate or citrate-derived acetate groups), respectively, on the surface of the QDs (Karaçetin, Sivrikaya, & Imamoğlu, 2014). The additional infrared absorption peak at 3200 cm−1 indicated the presence of OH groups on the surface of the Zn-doped CQDs. The high solubility of Zn-doped CQDs in aqueous solution was caused by the citrate, acetate and hydroxyl groups on the surface of QDs which imparted high hydrophilicity.

In this work, we aimed to use two distinct strategies to boost the SERS response to kanamycin. The first was to couple the longitudinal SPR of the Au nanorods with the Raman excitation laser (785 nm), thus maximizing plasmonic enhancement (i.e. local electric field) effects. The second strategy was to use the Zn-doped CQDs as a catalytic switch controlled by an aptamer. In order to verify the feasibility ofexperimentalstrategy, Raman spectra were collected for aqueous solutions of kanamycin (10−8 g mL−1) under three sets of testing conditions: 1) catalytic reaction system (GLC + HAuCl4 + QDs + aptamer), 2) catalytic reaction system + kanamycin (GLC + HAuCl4 + QDs + aptamer + kanamycin) and 3) catalytic reaction system + kanamycin + gold nanorods (GLC + HAuCl4 + QDs + aptamer + kanamycin + gold nanorods). As shown in Fig. S3, each of the amplification strategies boosted the SERS signal (kanamycin). Intuitively, in the absence of kanamycin, no SERs signal for that molecule was observed. Under such conditions, the aptamer was wound on the surface of the Zn-doped CQDs, and the switch was in the “off” state. In the presence of kanamycin, the aptamer attached to the kanamycin, thus enabling the catalytic activity of the Zn-doped QDs to be exploited in Au3+ reduction on the Au nanorods. At this point, the switch was in the “on” state and the Raman intensity of the SERs signals increased greatly, indicating that this doubly-enhanced signal SERS strategy was feasible for the ultrasensitive detection of kanamycin.

We should point out at this stage that the signals detected in our SERS spectra were not due to kanamycin. Here were used 4-MPBA as the Raman label in the detection process. After adding 4-MPBA, two characteristic peaks appeared at 1060 and 495 cm−1. Although the Raman intensity at 1060 cm−1 was larger,the stability and the linear relationship between the Raman intensity and the concentration were not as good as for the peak at 495 cm−1, so the Raman signal at 495 cm−1 was used for quantification purposes.

In order to optimize the overall sensitivity of the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor, some experimental conditions needed to be optimized. The aldehyde groups of GLC are reductive, thus enabling HAuCl4 reduction at Au0 upon the AuNRs. In order to improve the sensitivity of the sensor, the concentration of HAuCl4 and GLC present in the catalytic reaction were investigated, and the results were shown in Fig. 3 (A) and Fig. 3(B), respectively. When the concentration of GLC was 0.05 mol L−1, the Raman intensity increased with increasing HAuCl4 concentration up to 15 μmol L−1. The optimal GLC concentration was determined to be 0.1 mol L−1. Accordingly, HAuCl4 and GLC concentrations of 15 μmol L−1 and 0.1 mol L−1 were used in the SERS determination of kanamycin.

Next, the amount of the aptamer was optimized with the results shown in Fig. 3(C). The intensity of Raman signals increased with the amount of aptamer increasing, then remain constant (200 μL aptamer). Thus, 200 μL was selected as the optimum amount of aptamer.

The oxidation–reduction reaction between GLC and HAuCl4 is sensitive to temperature, thus the effect of reaction temperature on the performance of the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method was investigated. As shown in Fig. S4, the Raman intensity at 495 cm−1 gradually increased as the temperature increased up to 65 °C, then abruptly decreased at higher temperatures due to thermal decomposition/degradation of the aptamer. Based on these results, 65 °C was selected as the optimum reaction temperature.

Finally, the reaction time of the catalytic switch controlled by the aptamer was optimized. Fig. S5 shows that the reaction had proceeded to completion after 30 min. Therefore, 30 min was selected as the optimum reaction time in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor, featuring Zn-doped CQDs catalytic switch controlled by the nucleic acid aptamer and size-optimized gold nanorods, evaluated under the fully optimized testing conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 , a good linear relationship was established between the Raman intensity and the negative logarithm of the kanamycin concentration in the concentration range of 10−12-10−5 g mL−1. The linear regression equation was Y = -3366.4X + 40808 (R2 = 0.9932), where X is the negative logarithm of the kanamycin concentration. The limit of detection (LOD, whichwas calculated on the base of signal to noise ratio (S/N = 3)) of the method was 3.03 × 10−13 g mL−1, which was lower than that of the reported methods for the detecting kanamycin (Table S1). In addition, this method had good selectivity due to the specific recognition ability of aptamers. The detection limit of this method was also much lower than the maximum residue limits of kanamycin in animal-derived foods stipulated by China (150 μg kg−1), Japan (40 μg kg−1) and the European Union (150 μg kg−1) (Wei et al., 2012). Therefore, this method was sensitive enough to be used in real world samples for kanamycin detection.

The specificity of the method was evaluated by comparing the Raman intensity of the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor toward kanamycin, gentamicin, amikacin and vancomycin. It could be seen from Fig. 5 that Raman signals in the presence of the aminoglycoside antibiotics (gentamicin, amikacin) and one polypeptide antibiotic (vancomycin) were similar to that in DDW, and far weaker than those in the presence of kanamycin. This is explained by gentamicin, amikacin and vancomycin not specifically binding to the aptamer. The results confirm that the SERS method developed was highly selective for kanamycin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accuracy and applicability of the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the accuracy of the sensor, six potentially interfering substances commonly found in food matrices (ascorbic acid, glycine, l-cysteine, Cu2+, Mg2+, K+) were selected for the interference experiments. The results in Fig. S6 show that the sensor had good anti-interference characteristics.

The applicability of the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method wasinvestigated through experiments using kanamycin-spiked milk, milkpowder and honey samples. Spiking concentrations were 0.05 mg kg−1, 0.2 mg kg−1 and 2 mg kg−1, respectively. The results were summarized in Table 1 . For each concentration, measurements were performed in triplicate.Good recoveries for kanamycin were obtained (84.1%-107.2%) in all tests, with the relative standard deviation being less than 2.8%.

In order to verify the practicability of the method, the content of kanamycin in three kinds of milks (milk 1, milk 2 and milk 3) were determined by HPLC and the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method, respectively. The contents of kanamycin in the three milks detected by HPLC were 0 μg kg−1 (milk 1), 94.483 μg kg−1 (milk 2) and 19.807 μg kg−1 (milk 3, Fig. S7), respectively. The corresponding kanamycin levels by the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method were 0 μg kg−1, 95.960 and 19.464 μg kg−1, respectively. There was no significant difference between the results of the two methods (P > 0.05), indicating that the doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method had high accuracy and could be used for the detection of kanamycin in actual food samples.

A doubly-enhanced signal SERS sensor method was developed for the detection of kanamycin in foods and beverages. The sensor harnessed the plasmonic properties of size-controlled gold nanorods, the catalytic properties of Zn-doped CQDs and an aptamer control switch for SERS signal enhancement. Under optimized testing conditions, the sensor offered high sensitivity, good specificity and a wide linear detection range for kanamycin detection and quantification, with a LOD ~ 3.03 × 10−13 g mL−1. The sensor was successfully applied for kanamycin detection in spiked milk and honey. Simply by changing the sequence of nucleic acid aptamers, the sensor should be easily adaptable for the detection of other harmful substances in food products.

Ximo Wang: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Chen Chen: Visualization, Investigation, Software, Validation. Geoffrey I.N. Waterhouse: Writing - review & editing. Xuguang Qiao: Supervision. Zhixiang Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Key R & D Project of Shandong Province (No. 2019GNC106030) and Key R & D Project of Jining (2019NYNS002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.130261.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
